course_title: Realism, Fiction, and Factuality on Screen
course_description:
This course examines a range of theoretical approaches to the issues of realism, fiction, and factuality across various kinds of film, TV, and screen media. In an era of increasing anxiety about fakery, the blurring lines between fact and fiction, and the nature of viewer belief, this course will give students a deeper understanding of past and current ways of thinking about the screen's relationship to realities.

This course is the third year compulsory course for students taking the Film and Television Studies major. It examines older and newer theoretical approaches to the question of how films and television programs achieve and evoke experiences of reality for viewers; the relationship between the real world and the screen; and the strategies film and TV creatorsﾠhave used to tell stories about real people and events. As such, it considers fictional and factual forms, computer generated imagery, animation, documentaries, docudramas and biopics, and draws upon classical film theory, feminist film theory, animation theory, documentary theory, phenomenological theory and reception studies.

course_aims:
1. To ensure that students complete their Film and Television major with a thorough understanding of some of the major recent theoretical and critical approaches in the field.

2. To facilitate students' integration of skills and knowledge gained in prior Film and Television Studies courses, as well as to distill and ﾠilluminate key skills,ﾠ concepts and methods emphaisized in the major

3. To help students make connections between concepts and debates in the scholarly study of film/television and those in popular discourse; more broadly, to encourage students to think about how the skills and knowledge developed in humanities disciplines like Film and Television Studies are of value beyond academia.

4. To assist students to further develop the ability to read carefully, think critically, and write and speak clearly.

learning_outcomes.description: Apply and test some of the key theoretical arguments in Film and TV Studies about relationships between the screen and the material, social, historical, and experiential aspects of the world.
Analyse realism, fiction, and truth in film and animation practices across different cultures and historical contexts, and discuss them clearly and with sensitivity.
Assess the impact of new technologies on practices of film and TV realism, and distinguish among different kinds of film/TV, their intentions, and potential impacts.
Explain ethical concerns about the responsibilities of film and TV producers to their audiences and those who images they use, and identify where these concerns do and don’t apply.
Closely analyse films and/or television programs, to produce evidence for arguments communicated via speech, writing, or video essay.
Synthesize and apply complex ideas about film and TV, and communicate them in compelling, meaningful ways to a target audience.

assessment_details.title: Seminar participation/engagement
assessment_details.task_description: One mark is available each week for seminar participation/engagement, for a total of ten marks over ten weeks. To receive the mark each week: write your name on the class sign-in sheet in the seminar room AND EITHER speak into the microphone (raise your hand, and I will bring it to you), OR write a response to a prompt or question on the seminar slides (or ask questions about the seminar topic) in the discussion space on blackboard. Discussion posts must engage with specific points from the seminar. Broad statements about a topic that do not refer to something specific from the seminar will receive a ‘0’. If you are unable to attend the seminar due to a timetable clash, illness, or other good reason, email the course co-ordinator, Lisa, and she will arrange an alternative engagement activity so you can still earn the mark. It is your responsibility to ensure your name is recorded on the sign-in sheet in class so that your participation can be marked, or email Lisa l.bode@uq.edu.au if you cannot make it to class. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
In-class: sign the attendance sheet in the seminar room, and either speak into the microphone during the seminar or post in the discussion space on Blackboard. The Blackboard discussion for the week closes at midnight on the day of the seminar.
If you are unable to attend the seminar due to a timetable clash, illness, or other good reason, email the course co-ordinator, Lisa, and she will arrange an alternative engagement activity with a due date.
It is your responsibility to ensure your name is recorded on the sign-in sheet in class so that your participation can be marked, or email Lisa l.bode@uq.edu.au if you cannot make it to class.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Email Lisa directly for your options if you are unable to attend the seminar.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
If student does not attend and participate/engage with seminar using microphone or discussion post, or if student does not reach out to course coordinator to arrange alternative task for a missed week, no marks will be award for this task.

assessment_details.title: In-class writing tasks
assessment_details.weight: 40% 5 marks x 10 weeks. Best 8 for final mark out of 40
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Each week in tutorials (starting week 2) there will be a timed writing exercise that must be completed on the provided worksheet (you are responsible for bringing your own pen or pencil). Students are not permitted to access devices of any kind during the timed task, but may consult printed or handwritten notes. You will be given 15 minutes preparation time, and 15 minutes writing time. The exercises will be different each week. Earlier ones will be concerned with applying that week’s concept to a screen case study. The later ones will be focused on generating ideas and drafting parts of the final assignment (the screening program or video essay with written introduction). This gives you the option to complete part of the work for the final assignment in workshops, receive marks for that draft work, as well as receive feedback as you progress. Feedback will be given informally. Your tutor will collect the worksheets afterwards to record completion of the assessment task. Each writing task will be marked out of 5. Your best 8 workshop exercises will count towards your final mark out of 40. To check this grade during the semester, please ask your tutor. Students who miss a number of classes due to illness or other factors beyond their control will be given a chance to do a makeup session in week 13. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
in-class writing tasks are to be completed in person each week in class, using paper and pen, and submitted to the tutor at the end of the class.
Students who miss a number of classes due to illness or other factors beyond their control will be given a chance to do a makeup session later in the semester.
students who do not complete any writing tasks under these conditions will not receive any of the available marks for this assessment.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Students who miss a number of classes due to illness or other factors beyond their control will be given a chance to do a makeup session later in the semester. As this is a secure in-person assessment, there is no option to submit the work online. Students must ensure they come to class and do the task there, or if this is not possible, they must monitor their emails to find out when the makeup session is scheduled so they can make up the work they have missed.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.weight: 50% 2500 words for screening program or 10 mins video essay plus 1000 words written introduction

assessment_details.task_description: This assignment requires you to demonstrate your engagement with at least two key theoretical concepts from the course; synthesise and apply them to a variety of screen works; and develop an argument. You have a choice of two assignment formats: an analytical video essay with written introduction; or program notes for a hypothetical curated screening program or film festival. More information about this assignment will be available on Blackboard. You will also have the opportunity to develop your work for this assignment in the tutorials through the in-class writing tasks, and receive feedback and marks along the way. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
TurnItIn Receipts:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
EchoVideo Submission
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using EchoVideo . Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your EchoVideo .
When you successfully submit your assessment to EchoVideo, you will see text confirming your submission is complete. You will also receive an email confirmation message.
Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Note that if you are planning to graduate at the end of semester one, a lengthy extension for this final assignment may lead to a delay in your final grade upload, which may in turn block your ability to graduate at the mid-year ceremony.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Introduction What do we mean when we use words like 'realism' or 'authenticity'' (or 'unrealistic', or 'inauthentic') to talk about films and TV shows? In what ways do these things seem to matter to how we value or devalue films and TV? How do we distinguish between fiction and non-fiction and what happens when these categories are mixed up? This is the first seminar. We will discuss the aims and objectives of the course, introduce the main concepts, and explain the assessment. See Blackboard (e-learning site) for more details. Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 2: Realism(s): Matters of screen style In the first part of the course we examine fictional film and TV's various relationships to realism and reality. We start this week with screen production: what is realism in film production. How has 'realism' in filmmaking and screen production been defined in different places and times? When has realism just been about preserving an illusion? What have been seen as some of the 'stakes' of realism on screen? See Blackboard (e-learning site) for required reading and viewing. seminar participation/engagement is marked this week. In-class writing exercises start this week in the tutorials. Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 3: Realist Film Theories and the Camera We turn from production (what filmmakers do to make their works seem 'real') to how film theorists have argued about the medium of film and its special relationship to reality. Why have the film camera and the photographic image been so central to realist film theories? How and why did these theories emerge? When is the camera's recording of reality most meaningful in our viewing of film and TV? What if anything is still useful about these theories for us in the age of computer generated images (CGI) and AI generated imagery? See Blackboard for what to read and what to watch. seminar participation/engagement is marked this week. In class writing task in tutoral. Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 4: The sensory and emotional realities of the viewing experience This week we look at the reality of the viewing experience, or how and why we respond 'as if it were real'. Most of us have cried or felt angry or exuberant watching a film. Some of us have thrown up. You may find your body making involuntary movements or felt the hairs prick up on your arms while watching the screen. How does this work? We often use words like 'visceral', 'sensuous', 'electrifying', 'overwhelming' when talking about films - for they are indeed not just stories told with moving images and sound on a 2D screen, but sensory and emotional experiences . How have phenomenologists and cognitive theorists of films tried to explain what happens in our bodies and minds when we watch? How have particular filmmakers had an interest in making films that we watch not just with our eyes and ears but our emotions, and our whole bodies? See Blackboard for what to read and watch. seminar participation/engagement is marked this week. In class writing task in tutoral. Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 5: Feminist Approaches to the social and political realities of film Guest Seminar from Dr Bonnie Evans. This week we look at some of the arguments about film's impact upon or relation to social and political reality, with a turn to feminist film theory. If we consider that This week's lens is feminist film theory (and practice) - which has been hugely influential on cultural discourse about gender as well as the work of filmmakers. See Blackboard for what to watch and read. seminar participation/engagement is marked this week. In class writing task in tutoral. Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 6 Animation Aesthetics: From Realism to Abstraction Film and TV is more than live action. It includes animation! But animation has often been neglected in film studies, in part because the early domination of realist film theory argued that the photographic image defined film as a medium (and therefore, animation was pushed to the margins - not 'really' film). Animation theorists though argue that animation has a range of really productive and interesting ways of emulating or commenting upon reality. It comprises a wide variety of styles, methods, and approaches. There is always a stylistic choice in how animation's bodies, spaces, and movements look. How has animation been theorised in relation to reality, and how has realism been defined in animation? How has it been discussed in relation to different modes, and in what ways are animation's various departures from realism seen to be central to its meanings? See Blackboard for what to watch and read. seminar participation/engagement is marked this week. In class writing task in tutoral. Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 7: Documenting reality, claiming truth. This is the first week of the second half of the course - we turn from realism/reality on screen to the related question of truth and factuality on screen. Today we consider documentaries: what is a documentary? How and why has the definition of documentary changed over time? How do we differentiate between a documentary and other forms of media that claim to tell truths about the world? How do documentaries persuade us, what do documentaries do with evidence and rhetoric, and what approaches can they take to knowledge? See Blackboard for what to watch and read. seminar participation/engagement is marked this week. In class writing task in tutoral. Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 8: Animating realities and expressing truths in animated documentary This week we examine animation in documentaries and animated documentary. If animation creates its own realities (worlds that exist only as animation), how can it also be used to help us understand our own realities or those of others? We look at how animation has been used in factual contexts to explain, express, and interrogate real events and experiences, communicate facts and data, and communicate things that cannot be captured by a camera. See Blackboard for what to watch and read. seminar participation/engagement is marked this week. In class writing task in tutoral. Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 9: Biopics and Docudrama Guest seminar by Dr Melanie Piper This week we look at how biopics turn real people into characters and docudrama turn real events into stories. what are the functions and fascinations of these forms of "based on a true story" screen media? how are they different from documentaries? how do they straddle the lines between factuality and fiction? what are their ethical dimensions? See Blackboard for what to watch and read. seminar participation/engagement is marked this week. In class writing task in tutoral. Sub-activity: Tutorial
Week 10: Monday Public Holiday (NO CLASS) Labour Day Public Holiday. No Classes this week for this course.

Week 11: Deepfakes and the uses and misuses of real people's images and lives Deepfakes have generated a lot of anxiety and discussion about the ethics of using other people's images - about appropriation, recontextualisation, power, play, ethics, and harms. How do we decide what is a legitimate, creative use of someone's likeness, and what is an unethical or harmful use? How might this connect to other ways in which film and TV creators use the images and stories of others, such as in true crime or biopics? See Blackboard for what to watch and read. seminar participation/engagement is marked this week. In class writing task in tutoral. Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 12: Ambiguous truths, deceptions, satire, and AI Generated video In this final topic we examine screen works that have ambiguous or playful relationships to truth, history, and memory, and consider how AI tools (for colorisation of black and white film, dubbing, for instance) and AI Generated vintage video may be fundamentally transforming our relationship to the audio-visual archive and our perceptions of the past. Do we still care about what is real/fake, true/fabrication, and, if so, why, and how do we keep the distinction alive? To prepare, please watch: My Winnipeg (Guy Maddin, 2007) or 24 Hour Party People (Michael Winterbottom, 2002). Please check Blackboard for readings. Participation/engagement will be graded this week (last one). Sub-activity: Tutorial

Week 13: NO CLASS Students who have had documented absences from tutorials during the semester (and have been sending documentation to the course coordinator for those weeks) and have missed in-class writing tasks will be able to do a make-up session this week.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
